content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

July 23, 1997

Guy D. Petzold
Deputy City Attorney
City of Stockton
Office of the City Attorney
425 North El Dorado Street
Stockton, California 95202

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-97-212

Dear Mr. Petzold:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you have requested general advice regarding
application of the law, rather than advice on behalf of a specific public official or based upon
specific facts, we are rendering informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. Does Proposition 208's prohibition on appointees to boards and commissions
contributing to or soliciting or accepting contributions for the person who made the appointment
apply only to the councilmember who made the nomination or to the entire city council which
ratified the appointment?

2. Does the prohibition apply to contributions of time spent by a planning commissioner
who volunteers to work on a city councilmember's campaign?

___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329, subd. (c)(3).)

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. I-97-212
Page 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Since each city councilmember only nominates and a majority vote of the entire city
council is necessary to make the appointment, the prohibition applies to the entire city council.
Therefore an appointed member of the planning commission may not contribute to, or solicit or
accept contributions for any of the members of the city council.

2. Volunteer personal services constitute an exception to the definition of "contribution"
and therefore an appointed planning commissioner may volunteer personal time to work for the
campaign of a city councilmember. However, the planning commissioner's volunteer activities
are limited by Section 85705, which prohibits the planning commissioner from soliciting or
accepting contributions on behalf of any councilmember's campaign committee.

FACTS

The City of Stockton appoints the members of its planning commission as follows: each
councilmember nominates a person for planning commissioner from his or her district. Each
nominee becomes a planning commissioner upon ratification by a majority vote of the city
council.

Article XXIV, Section 2400 of the city charter, regarding appointment of the city
planning commission, states as follows:

"There shall be a City Planning Commission composed of a number of members
equal to the number of members on the City Council including the Mayor. Each
member of the City Council shall select a candidate from the district from which
the Councilmember has been elected to be nominated for the position of Planning
Commissioner. The Mayor shall nominate an at-large member of the Planning
Commission. Each nominee shall become a member of the Planning Commission
upon ratification by a majority vote of the City Council."

ANALYSIS

Question 1

Section 85705, which was added to the Act by Proposition 208, provides as follows:

"No person appointed to a public board or commission or as Trustee of the
California State University or Regent of the University of California during tenure
in office shall donate to, or solicit or accept any campaign contribution for, any
committee controlled by the person who made the appointment to that office or
any other entity with the intent that the recipient of the donation be any committee
controlled by such person who made the appointment."

File No. I-97-212
Page 3

Although there is no definition of "public board or commission" in Section 85705, the
planning commission is a public board which serves a public function regarding planning issues
in the City of Stockton. Planning commissioners are specifically mentioned in the Act as public
officials who by definition occupy such a position of public trust that they are required by statute
to file statements of economic interests with full disclosure. (Section 87200; Vasquez-Connolly
Advice Letter, No. A-97-181.)

Each member of the planning commission is selected and nominated by an individual
member of the city council, but a majority vote of the entire city council is necessary to ratify the
appointment. "Person" as defined in the Act means not only an individual but also "any
organization or group of persons acting in concert." (Section 82047.) Therefore, the "person
who made the appointment," as referred to in Section 85705, is the entire city council since each
city councilmember nominates a potential planning commissioner and a majority vote of the
council is necessary to ratify the appointment. Thus, no person appointed to the planning
commission may donate to, solicit or accept any campaign contribution for any committee
controlled by any member of the city council. (Vasquez-Connolly, supra, and Abdelnour Advice
Letter, No. A-97-164, copies enclosed.)

We have reached the opposite conclusion in situations where the mayor appoints an
individual to a public board or commission and the appointment is subject to ratification by the
city council. We have concluded that the mayor is the only person who made the appointment
and to whom the prohibition in Section 85705 applies. (Abdelnour Advice Letter, supra, Lyons
Advice Letter, No. A-97-203, copies enclosed.) Since, under your facts, each councilmember
merely nominates a candidate, the nominating councilmember is not making the appointment.

Question 2

This question refers to a situation where a planning commissioner volunteers his or her
personal time to work on a city councilmember's campaign and does activities such as precinct
walking, handing out flyers, making signs, etc. It may also include helping with fundraisers, but
not soliciting funds on behalf of the councilmember.

Volunteer personal services are specifically excluded from the definition of
"contribution." (Section 82015 and Regulation 18215(c)(2).) Therefore, they are not reportable
contributions and do not count toward the contribution limits. (Marenco Advice Letter,
No. A-96-373, question 7, copy enclosed.)

A planning commissioner may volunteer his or her personal time to work on a
councilmember's campaign, even though the planning commissioner is prohibited from making a
contribution to the councilmember under Section 85705, provided that the services do not
involve soliciting or accepting contributions. (Vasquez-Connolly, supra.) Section 85705 also
prohibits the planning commissioner from soliciting or accepting contributions on behalf of any
councilmember's campaign committee; the planning commissioner may not act as an
intermediary. (See Section 85702.)

File No. I-97-212
Page 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jill [illegible]
Stecher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:tls

Enclosures
